content.text: Errors Are a Normal Part of Programming

Every program encounters unexpected situations — a file that doesn't exist, a network connection that drops, a user who types text where a number was expected. Without a way to detect and respond to these situations, a program simply crashes. Error and exception handling is the mechanism that lets code recognize when something goes wrong, respond gracefully, and continue running (or fail in a controlled, informative way).
is a problem that occurs during a program's execution. An
is the formal object that represents that error — it carries information about what went wrong, where it happened, and why.

Most modern programming languages treat exceptions as first-class objects that can be "thrown" (raised) when something goes wrong, and "caught" (handled) by code that knows how to deal with that specific problem.
There are three broad categories of errors:
— The code itself is malformed and cannot even be parsed by the interpreter or compiler. These are caught before the program runs. Example: a missing closing bracket.
— The code is syntactically valid but fails during execution. Example: dividing by zero, or accessing an index that doesn't exist.
— The code runs without crashing but produces the wrong result. Exception handling does not catch these; they require debugging and testing.
Exception handling deals almost entirely with
Without exception handling, a single unexpected input or missing file would terminate the entire program abruptly. This is unacceptable in most real-world software:
A web server that crashes on one bad request would take down service for all users.
A data pipeline that stops on one malformed record would lose all subsequent data.
An AI application that fails when an API call times out would give the user no feedback.
with a useful message when recovery is impossible

In Generative AI applications specifically, exception handling is critical because these programs interact with external APIs (like OpenAI or Anthropic), process unpredictable user input, handle large data streams, and run long inference pipelines — all situations where failures are common and recovery logic is essential.
The fundamental mechanism across almost all languages is the
block and starts executing code normally.
block stops immediately at that line.
block runs instead of crashing the program.
Python's full exception handling structure has four clauses:
— The block of code to attempt.
— Catches a specific type of exception. Multiple
clauses handle different error types. The
syntax captures the exception object so its message can be read.
block completed without raising any exception. Useful for code that should only execute on success.
— Always runs, regardless of whether an exception occurred. Used for cleanup: closing files, releasing connections, freeing resources.
Python organizes all built-in exceptions into an inheritance hierarchy. Understanding this hierarchy helps write precise, targeted exception handlers.

BaseException
 ├── SystemExit
 ├── KeyboardInterrupt
 └── Exception
      ├── ArithmeticError
      │    ├── ZeroDivisionError
      │    └── OverflowError
      ├── LookupError
      │    ├── IndexError
      │    └── KeyError
      ├── TypeError
      ├── ValueError
      ├── IOError / OSError
      │    └── FileNotFoundError
      ├── RuntimeError
      │    └── RecursionError
      └── AttributeError
Catching a parent class catches all its children.
, which is almost never what you want — it would prevent Ctrl+C from stopping a script.
exception type you can handle, before catching general ones.
Code can raise exceptions deliberately using the
keyword. This is how you signal to the caller that something went wrong.
Re-raising an exception (passing it up the call stack) is done with a bare
This produces a traceback that shows both the original
For any non-trivial application, defining custom exception types is a best practice. It makes error handling more precise and meaningful.
Now callers can handle these at exactly the right level of specificity:

Program calls a function
        │
        ▼
  Function executes
        │
   Error occurs?
   ┌────┴────┐
   │ YES     │ NO
   ▼         ▼
Exception  Continue
is raised  normally
   │
   ▼
Is there a matching
  except clause?
   ┌────┴────┐
   │ YES     │ NO
   ▼         ▼
Run except  Exception
  block     propagates
   │        up the call
   ▼        stack
Run finally    │
  block        ▼
   │       Reaches top
   ▼       of call stack?
Continue       │
program        ▼
           Program crashes
           with traceback
When an exception is not caught in the current function, it automatically propagates up through every function in the
until something catches it or the program terminates. This propagation is what makes it possible to handle errors at the appropriate level — not every function needs to handle every error.
Interacting with external resources (files, databases, APIs, network)
Processing user-provided input that may be malformed
Calling functions that are documented to raise exceptions
Writing library code where callers need to handle your errors
Performing operations that have meaningful failure modes (parsing, type conversion)
Do not use exception handling for:
Normal control flow. Using exceptions to
out of loops or signal ordinary conditions is an anti-pattern in most languages (Python has one notable exception:
Validating data that should be validated with an
Hiding bugs. Catching all exceptions broadly and silently swallowing them makes bugs impossible to find.
Example 3 — Retry Logic for API Calls
A production Generative AI pipeline that processes user questions and generates answers might look like this:

import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

class LLMPipelineError(Exception):
    pass

def generate_answer(user_question: str, context_docs: list) -> str:
    """
    Generate an answer to a user question using an LLM.
    Handles all failure modes with appropriate recovery logic.
    """
    # Step 1: Validate inputs
    if not user_question or not user_question.strip():
        raise ValueError("User question cannot be empty")

    # Step 2: Build the prompt
    try:
        prompt = build_prompt(user_question, context_docs)
    except TemplateError as e:
        logger.error(f"Prompt construction failed: {e}")
        raise LLMPipelineError("Failed to build prompt") from e

    # Step 3: Call the LLM with retry logic
    try:
        response = call_api_with_retry(prompt, max_retries=3)
    except RateLimitError:
        logger.warning("Rate limit exceeded after retries")
        return "The service is temporarily busy. Please try again in a moment."
    except APIError as e:
        logger.error(f"LLM API call failed: {e}", exc_info=True)
        raise LLMPipelineError("LLM call failed") from e

    # Step 4: Parse and validate the response
    try:
        answer = parse_response(response)
    except (KeyError, ValueError) as e:
        logger.error(f"Response parsing failed: {e}. Raw response: {response}")
        raise LLMPipelineError("Invalid response format from LLM") from e

    return answer
This pipeline demonstrates several production patterns:
block handling only the errors relevant to that step.
All unexpected errors are logged with
Errors are wrapped in domain-specific exceptions (
The rate limit case returns a user-friendly message instead of crashing.
Input validation happens before any expensive operations.
2. Using exceptions for normal control flow

# BAD
try:
    value = my_dict["key"]
except KeyError:
    value = "default"

# BETTER — use dict.get() which is designed for this
value = my_dict.get("key", "default")
3. Losing the original exception (destroying the traceback)
4. Putting too much code inside the
block contains twenty lines, any one of them could raise the exception. Keep
blocks tight — include only the line(s) that might fail.

# BAD
try:
    data = fetch_data()
    cleaned = clean(data)
    result = process(cleaned)
    save(result)
    send_notification(result)
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Something failed: {e}")   # No idea which step

# BETTER — each risky step gets its own handling
data = fetch_data()           # Let this propagate if it fails
cleaned = clean(data)
try:
    result = process(cleaned)
except ProcessingError as e:
    logger.error(f"Processing failed: {e}")
    raise

# BAD — if an error occurs, the file is never closed
f = open("data.txt")
data = f.read()   # What if this raises?
f.close()

# GOOD — use a context manager which calls close() in its __exit__
with open("data.txt") as f:
    data = f.read()
Catch the narrowest exception type that makes sense.
If you catch an exception to log it and then re-raise, use
in the log call to capture the full traceback.
Any resource that must be released (file handles, database connections, network sockets) belongs in a
block or, better, a context manager.
Define a custom exception hierarchy early.
In any application beyond a simple script, create a base
class and subclass it for each distinct failure domain. This allows callers to handle errors at exactly the right level.
block is almost always a bug waiting to happen. If silence is genuinely intentional, document it with a comment and at minimum log the exception at
checks to reject bad input before expensive operations. Reserve exception handling for truly unexpected failures, not foreseeable bad input.
Code that should run only when no exception occurred belongs in the
clause, not at the bottom of the
block. This makes the success path explicit.
Prefer EAFP over LBYL in Python.

Python culture favors "Easier to Ask Forgiveness than Permission" — try the operation and handle the exception — over "Look Before You Leap" — checking conditions before acting. This is idiomatic Python, though the right choice depends on context.
Exception handling has a cost, but it is almost always negligible compared to the operations being protected (file I/O, network calls, database queries). The key performance considerations are:
In the happy path (no exception), a
in CPython. The interpreter sets up a handler but doesn't pay significant cost unless an exception actually occurs.

, the interpreter must unwind the call stack, construct the exception object, and capture the traceback. This is measurably slower than normal execution — which is why exceptions should not be used for control flow in tight loops.

captures and formats the full traceback as a string, which has real cost. In high-throughput paths, consider logging only the exception message in the hot path and the full traceback only in slower error-handling paths.

, circuit breakers and bulkheads (patterns from distributed systems) extend exception handling to the architectural level — detecting when a downstream service is consistently failing and stopping calls to it rather than repeatedly raising exceptions.
— The correct tool for recording exceptions in production.
automatically includes the current exception and traceback.
— A context manager for explicitly and readably suppressing specific exceptions:

from contextlib import suppress
with suppress(FileNotFoundError):
    os.remove("temp_file.txt")   # Don't care if it doesn't exist
— Used to write custom context managers that handle setup and teardown with exception awareness.
— Provides utilities for extracting, formatting, and printing tracebacks programmatically.
— Production error monitoring tools that automatically capture, aggregate, and alert on exceptions in running applications. Essential for any production system.
— A Python library for adding retry logic with exponential backoff, jitter, and per-exception retry conditions. Far more powerful than writing retry loops by hand.
exceptions with detailed field-level messages when data doesn't match a schema. Widely used in AI and API applications for input validation.
Language-level mechanism for signaling and handling errors
Propagates automatically, widely understood, rich information
Can be overused; broad catches hide bugs
, or an error code on failure
Easy to ignore; pollutes every call site with checks
Functions return a value that is either success or error
Forces callers to handle errors; type-safe
Leads to "callback hell"; awkward in synchronous code
Python's exception system is the standard and idiomatic approach. Result types are sometimes emulated with libraries like
Avoid leaking sensitive information in exception messages.

If an exception message is shown to end users, it must not include stack traces, database queries, file paths, API keys, or internal system details. Log the full details server-side; show users only a generic, friendly message.
Exception handling does not replace input validation.
Security-sensitive inputs (user IDs, filenames, API parameters) should be validated and sanitized before use, not just wrapped in a
Unhandled exceptions in production should trigger alerts.
A spike in unhandled exceptions is an early warning sign of a bug, attack, or infrastructure issue. Monitoring tools like Sentry make this automatic.

If an attacker can craft an input that causes expensive exception processing in a tight loop, this can degrade service. Validate and reject malformed input at the boundary before it enters the processing pipeline.
Exceptions are objects that represent runtime errors; the
structure is how they are caught and handled.
Catch exceptions as specifically as possible — broad catches like
hide bugs and make debugging extremely difficult.
block always runs and is the correct place for cleanup code; context managers (
Raise custom exceptions using domain-specific classes so callers can handle different failure modes at exactly the right level.
to preserve the original cause and make tracebacks informative.

In Generative AI applications, exception handling around API calls, retry logic with exponential backoff, and structured logging of errors are not optional — they are essential for any production-grade system.
Never show internal exception details to end users; log them internally and return sanitized, user-friendly error messages.
